narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 53% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, LSAT score of 32, relatively low class deciles in Year 1 (2nd decile) and Year 3 (1st decile), upper-middle family income, being a full-time female student of White background. Compared to typical students—who average a 3.21 GPA, 36.7 LSAT, and higher deciles around the 5th or 6th—the model saw your academic markers as weaker signals of bar success. The strongest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score, as it fell notably below the norm and carried the most weight in the decision. Next in influence came your Year 1 decile, which suggested early struggles relative to peers, followed closely by your even lower Year 3 decile, reinforcing a pattern of underperformance. Your undergraduate GPA played a similar role, pushing the odds toward failure since it was below average, while your family income bracket had a moderate but still unfavorable pull. Less influential but still contributing were your racial background, gender, and full-time status, each nudging slightly toward the failure prediction compared to dataset patterns. Overall, no single factor dominated overwhelmingly, but the top academic ones—LSAT, early and later deciles, and GPA—ranked highest in shaping this outcome. While demographics like gender, race, and family income are fixed, you can realistically retake the LSAT to boost that key score, as it's one of the most changeable predictors. Strengthening future academic performance through targeted study habits could also help mimic higher deciles in any grad-level work. Finally, dedicating time to bar exam prep courses tailored for students with similar starting points might shift your real-world odds, giving you a stronger shot at admission next cycle. Keep pushing—you've got potential to turn this around.
